title: Can Vines or Ivy Damage a Florida Tree? What Homeowners Should Check First
strengths: Good homeowner intent; distinguishes light vines from woody canopy growth; warns against yanking attached vines; considers concealed decay, canopy weight, structures, power lines, herbicide misuse, and gradual removal.
weaknesses: No H1, decision table, authoritative sources, contextual internal links, direct service routes, current update date, vine-identification workflow, invasive-status check, poison-ivy caution, propagule containment, or qualified pesticide boundary.
ai_notes: Predictable Short Answer → effects → checks → warning signs → herbicide → professional help → takeaway sequence.
florida_notes: Separate native or low-impact vines, invasive canopy-smothering vines such as air potato, self-clinging ornamentals, poison ivy, and woody vines. Identification must precede control.
internal_link_opportunities: Florida tree identification, invasive plant pages, trunk cracks, conks, power-line safety, DIY trimming, trimming/removal services.
cta_recommendation: Use three routes: identify/manage low vines, qualified invasive-plant control, and tree assessment/pruning when vines conceal defects or reach the canopy. Utility first near lines.
image_prompt: Florida tree vine decision matrix comparing light vine, woody vine, air potato, poison ivy, canopy growth, hidden decay, cut-at-base sequence, herbicide label review, utility first, and arborist inspection.

title: Can You Cut Down a Tree on Your Own Property in Florida? What Homeowners Should Check First
strengths: Strong legal intent; broadens the issue beyond ownership to property line, HOA, easement, right-of-way, protected trees, wetlands, mangroves, documentation, utilities, access, and cleanup.
weaknesses: No H1, decision table, official linked sources, contextual links, direct service routes, current update date, address-first jurisdiction workflow, exact statutory definitions, documentation-versus-estimate distinction, or explicit legal boundary.
ai_notes: Long sequence of legal layers followed by risk signs, documentation, DIY, better questions, and takeaway; several points repeat without a single decision path.
florida_notes: Current section 163.045 narrowly defines covered residential property and specifies an onsite assessment and signed documentation for the unacceptable-risk pathway; the rewrite must not imply every owned tree is exempt.
internal_link_opportunities: Florida permit guide, statute explainer, hazardous-tree documentation, unauthorized-removal fines, HOA, boundary trees, wetlands/mangroves, removal service.
cta_recommendation: Verify parcel, jurisdiction, tree status, documentation, and written authority before requesting an authorized removal scope.

strengths: Useful seasonal intent; correctly says objective and severity matter more than month; covers deadwood, clearance, topping, lion-tailing, palms, stressed trees, wet access, rigging, and utilities.
weaknesses: No H1, table, authoritative sources, contextual links, direct service routes, current date, species/objective matrix, live-crown dose, fruit-tree exceptions, active-nest screen, weather stop conditions, named-storm timing, or emergency-work distinction.
ai_notes: Intro → Short Answer → reasonable work → avoid list → storm myth → palms → stress → oaks → wait → questions → takeaway repeats the same core point.
florida_notes: Classify dead/broken hazards, young-tree structure, clearance, mature live-limb reduction, palms, fruit trees, stressed trees, active wildlife, wet sites, and approaching tropical weather.

title: Can You Remove Surface Tree Roots Without Hurting the Tree?
strengths: Strongest tree-risk article in the package; already has an H1 and distinguishes feeder roots, structural roots, yard conflicts, movement signs, hardscape redesign, utilities, mulch, groundcover, fill, and removal-versus-cutting.
weaknesses: No decision table, authoritative source section, contextual links, direct service routes, current date, root-exposure workflow, cumulative root-loss concept, species/palm distinctions, air-spade role, site-design professional, or formal utility-locate sequence. The one-inch statement reads too universally.
ai_notes: Well organized but still follows Short Answer → causes → risks → root types → warning signs → hardscape → alternatives → questions → takeaway.
florida_notes: Reject a universal safe diameter or distance; evaluate root size, distance, tree size/species, lean, targets, prior loss, soil, hardscape objective, and redesign alternatives together.
internal_link_opportunities: how close to cut roots, air-spade pruning, root damage recovery, retaining-wall roots, driveway decisions, 811, removal versus pruning, removal service.
cta_recommendation: Route first to tree and site assessment, then hardscape redesign, controlled root work, or removal. Stump grinding is not the service for roots of a living tree.

title: Can You Replant in the Same Spot After Stump Grinding?
strengths: Strong practical planting intent; distinguishes turf, shrubs, ornamentals, and new trees; covers chips, remaining roots, settling, drainage, planting depth, offset planting, old stump size, soil preparation, and future use. Existing contextual links are useful.
weaknesses: No H1, decision table, authoritative source section, direct service routes, current date, cause-of-removal screen, right-tree/right-place selection, planting standards, utility locate, irrigation establishment plan, end-use specification, or role distinction between stump provider and planting professional.
ai_notes: Repetitive Short Answer → plant-type sections → waiting → chips → Florida factors → mistakes → questions → FAQ → takeaway; several answers restate that preparation matters more than waiting.
florida_notes: Classify lawn, bed, shrub, palm, small tree, and large shade tree outcomes. Grinding depth and chip removal must follow the next use; same-spot planting is a site-design decision, not a calendar rule.

title: Do Stump Killers Really Work, or Is Grinding Better?
strengths: Clear chemical-control-versus-physical-removal distinction; useful comparison table; considers regrowth, active yard use, access, utilities, sensitive plants, water, pets, children, replanting, chips, and grinding scope.
weaknesses: No H1, authoritative sources, contextual links, direct service routes, current date, product-category distinctions, label-is-law boundary, cut-stump timing, licensed-application scenarios, species identification, water restrictions, integrated control, or explicit rejection of household chemicals and salt.
ai_notes: The chemical-versus-grinding distinction is repeated in most sections, then repeated again in the table, choice list, questions, takeaway, and FAQ.
florida_notes: Separate decay accelerants from registered herbicides and homeowner label use from work requiring a licensed applicator. Species and desired outcome control the decision.

title: How Long Do Roots Live After a Tree Is Cut Down?
strengths: Useful PAA intent; separates root persistence from continued growth and discusses species resprouting, root size, grinding limits, settling, hardscape voids, replanting, mushrooms, utilities, and follow-up.
weaknesses: No H1, decision table, authoritative sources, contextual links, direct service routes, current date, distinction among living roots/dead woody roots/root channels, species-specific control, utility or sewer exceptions, hardscape-contractor boundary, local-settlement caution, or reliable timeline evidence. The simple timeline reads too predictively.
ai_notes: Many short sections repeat that roots remain and decay; timeline, follow-up, what-not-to-do, grinding benefits, takeaway, and FAQ restate earlier material.
florida_notes: Answer four separate questions: can roots still sprout, can they keep enlarging, how long woody roots can remain, and what yard changes can occur as they decay. Avoid a universal decay calendar.
internal_link_opportunities: stump killers, stump grinding, same-spot replanting, settling, pavers, invasive species, 811, stump grinding service.
cta_recommendation: Route regrowth to species-specific control, visible stump to grinding, hardscape settlement to the appropriate site contractor, and utility/sewer concerns to the responsible specialist.
